focus_areas: Auth & JWT (lib/insecurity.ts, routes/verify.ts, routes/login.ts) — RSA key embed, algo confusion, MD5 hashing
SQL/NoSQL Injection (routes/search.ts, routes/login.ts, routes/dataExport.ts, routes/orderHistory.ts) — raw query concatenation
File Upload & Path Traversal (routes/fileUpload.ts, routes/fileServer.ts, routes/profileImageFileUpload.ts) — zip slip, null byte bypass
Deserialization (routes/b2bOrder.ts, routes/fileUpload.ts, lib/insecurity.ts) — XML/YAML parsing, noent, eval sandbox
SSTI & Code Exec (routes/userProfile.ts, routes/chatbot.ts) — eval() on user input
Hardcoded Secrets (lib/insecurity.ts, routes/checkKeys.ts, routes/web3Wallet.ts, routes/nftMint.ts) — RSA key, HMAC, mnemonics, API keys
Broken Access Control & Info Disclosure (server.ts, routes/metrics.ts, routes/dataExport.ts) — auto-scaffolding, metrics leak, directory listing
CSRF & Business Logic (server.ts, routes/basket.ts, routes/payment.ts, routes/coupon.ts) — missing CSRF, Z85 coupon forging

findings.title: SQL injection via raw string interpolation in login query
findings.description: Route POST /rest/user/login at line 31 constructs a raw SQL query using untrusted req.body.email and req.body.password with string concatenation: `SELECT * FROM Users WHERE email = '${req.body.email || ''}' AND password = '${security.hash(req.body.password || '')}' AND deletedAt IS NULL`. An attacker can terminate the email string and inject arbitrary SQL. The input flows from HTTP body → login handler → sequelize.query() with no parameterization.
findings.exploit_scenario: Send POST /rest/user/login with body: {"email": "' OR 1=1 --", "password": "x"}. The injected clause OR 1=1 bypasses authentication. Alternatively, UNION SELECT can exfiltrate the Users table including all passwords and PII.
findings.recommendation: Replace raw sequelize.query() with parameterized query: sequelize.query('SELECT * FROM Users WHERE email = :email AND password = :passw AND deletedAt IS NULL', { replacements: { email: req.body.email, passw: security.hash(req.body.password) }, model: UserModel, plain: true })
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: raw SQL string concatenation from HTTP body parameter, no parameterization, no input sanitization that would prevent injection. Classic, well-known CWE-89 pattern.

findings.title: SQL injection via raw string interpolation in product search
findings.description: Route GET /rest/products/search at line 19 constructs a raw SQL query: `SELECT * FROM Products WHERE ((name LIKE '%${criteria}%' OR description LIKE '%${criteria}%') AND deletedAt IS NULL) ORDER BY name`. The criteria value comes from req.query.q (line 17), truncated at 200 chars but otherwise unsanitized. Search input flows directly into SQL via string concatenation.
findings.exploit_scenario: Send GET /rest/products/search?q=' UNION SELECT * FROM Users--'. The UNION returns all user credentials. The code at lines 22-36 actually checks the response for user email/password, confirming exfiltration is possible and used to solve a challenge.
findings.recommendation: Use Sequelize parameterized query: ProductModel.findAll({ where: { name: { [Op.like]: `%${criteria}%` }, description: { [Op.like]: `%${criteria}%` } }, paranoid: true })
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: raw SQL concatenation from URL query parameter. The challenge code (lines 22-36) in this same file confirms UNION-based exfiltration against the Users table is the intended exploit path.

findings.title: Ethereum wallet mnemonic phrase hardcoded in source code
findings.description: Route POST /rest/web3/submitKey at line 10 has the plaintext mnemonic 'purpose betray marriage blame crunch monitor spin slide donate sport lift clutch' in source code. Used to derive HDNodeWallet (line 11) and private key (line 12). Directly accessible to anyone who reads the source. Funds any wallet derived from this mnemonic.
findings.exploit_scenario: Any attacker can read checkKeys.ts, extract the mnemonic, derive the private key, and sweep all funds from the corresponding Ethereum wallet address. The mnemonic is also usable to sign arbitrary Ethereum transactions from this wallet.
findings.recommendation: Store the mnemonic in an environment variable (e.g., process.env.ETH_MNEMONIC) or a secrets manager. Never commit it to source code or include it in the shipped bundle.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: 24-word BIP39 mnemonic is literal in source at line 10. Fully deterministic key derivation from BIP39 ensures full wallet compromise.

findings.title: RSA private key hardcoded in source enabling JWT forgery
findings.description: Variable privateKey at line 17 contains a plaintext RSA private key used to sign all JWT tokens (line 50). The key is used in authorize() which issues tokens with RS256 algorithm. Any attacker who reads the source can forge tokens for any user identity.
findings.exploit_scenario: Read lib/insecurity.ts:17 to extract the RSA private key. Use it to sign a JWT with arbitrary claims (e.g., role: 'admin', email: 'attacker@evil.com') via jwt.sign() with algorithm RS256. Present this forged token to any route protected by isAuthorized() middleware. The server will verify the signature against the public key and accept the forged token.
findings.recommendation: Store the RSA private key in a secure KMS, environment variable, or file with restricted filesystem permissions. Load it at startup, never embed in source code.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: RSA private key is a literal PEM string in source at line 17, used for jwt.sign() at line 50 and verify() at line 51. The JWT challenge code (routes/verify.ts:114) confirms this is the intended JWT auth mechanism.

findings.title: JWT algorithm confusion: RS256 public key accepted as HS256 shared secret
findings.description: The isAuthorized() middleware at line 48 uses express-jwt with the public key as the secret. express-jult/jws.verify at line 51 calls jws.verify(token, publicKey) without restricting the algorithm header. An attacker can create an HS256-signed token using the public key as the HMAC secret. The server verifies it as RS256 but jws.verify with the same key as secret will accept HS256 tokens signed with that key.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Extract the public key from lib/insecurity.ts:16 (or /encryptionkeys/jwt.pub). 2. Create a JWT with alg=HS256, signing with the public key bytes as the HMAC secret. 3. Set the role claim to 'admin'. The express-jwt middleware accepts it because it uses publicKey as the secret, and HS256 with publicKey as shared secret produces a valid token.
findings.recommendation: Use express-jwt with algorithms: ['RS256'] to explicitly restrict acceptable algorithms. Add algorithm validation middleware that rejects tokens with alg=HS256 when the app expects RS256.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: express-jwtpassphrase uses publicKey at line 48. jws.verify at line 51 passes publicKey as the secret parameter without algorithm restriction. This is the well-known JWT algorithm confusion vulnerability where asymmetric keys can be misused as symmetric HMAC secrets.

findings.title: MD5 password hashing with no salt enables offline credential recovery
findings.description: The hash() function at line 37 uses crypto.createHash('md5') with no salt. passwords are hashed with this function (models/user.ts:73) and compared via the same hash. MD5 is fast and unsalted, making it trivial to perform offline dictionary/brute-force attacks or use precomputed rainbow tables.
findings.exploit_scenario: Extract hashed passwords from the SQLite Users table. Since all passwords use the same unsalted MD5 function, attackers can use rainbow tables or GPU-accelerated brute force to recover Plaintext passwords. The login route (routes/login.ts:31) hashes the input with the same function, confirming this is the auth comparison mechanism.
findings.recommendation: Replace crypto.createHash('md5') with bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2id. Add a unique random salt per user via bcrypt's built-in salt mechanism. Set appropriate work factor (e.g., bcrypt cost factor of 12+).
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: MD5 hash with no salt at line 37, used for password storage (user.ts:73) and login comparison. MD5 is cryptographically broken for password hashing; there is no salt, no work factor.

findings.title: Arbitrary JavaScript execution via eval() on user-controlled username
findings.description: Route GET /profile at line 59 executes eval(code) where code is derived from user.username. When a username matches the pattern '#{...}', the substring between # and } is passed to eval() (line 54). The username is set via POST /profile update (routes/updateUserProfile.ts:33), which accepts arbitrary input.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Set username to '#{}require('child_process').execSync('whoami')#' via POST /profile. 2. Visit GET /profile. The eval() executes the injected code, returning command output in the HTML response. With the server running as root or a privileged user, this enables RCE.
findings.recommendation: Remove the eval() call entirely. Do not use user input in eval(). If template interpolation is needed, use a safe templating engine like Pug's built-in escaping or handlebars with auto-escaping. The Pug template at line 84 already handles this safely for normal cases.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: eval(code) at line 59 with code directly from user.username at line 54. The string pattern '#{...}' at line 52 is a clear code injection vector. This is a textbook CWE-95 eval injection.

findings.title: XXE injection via libxmljs with noent option in B2B XML upload
findings.description: Route POST /b2b/v2/orders receives XML uploads. At line 80, libxml.parseXml() is called with options { noblanks: true, noent: true, nocdata: true }. The noent: true option expands external entities, allowing an attacker to craft a malicious XML document that reads arbitrary server files or performs SSRF.
findings.exploit_scenario: Send a POST /b2b/v2/orders with XML body containing: <!DOCTYPE foo [<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM 'file:///etc/passwd'>]><root>&xxe;</root>. The noent option expands the entity, and the parsed XML string is returned in the error message (line 84), leaking the file contents to the attacker.
findings.recommendation: Set noent: false in the parseXml() options. Disable external entity processing entirely: libxml.parseXml(data, { noblanks: true, noent: false, nocdata: true }). Use a whitelist-based XML parser that does not process entities.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: noent: true at line 80 explicitly enables external entity expansion. The parsed result is returned in the HTTP response (line 84), confirming the data flows back to the attacker. This is the classic XXE configuration.

findings.title: finale-rest auto-scaffolding exposes full CRUD on all models
findings.description: Lines 445-460 of server.ts define autoModels with 14 Sequelize models exposed via finale-rest auto-scaffolding. Each model gets endpoints at /api/{Name}s and /api/{Name}s/:id. While some endpoints have denyAll() or isAuthorized(), the auto-generated CRUD endpoints provide bulk read/write access across Products, Challenges, Cards, Addresses, and other sensitive data models.
findings.exploit_scenario: The auto-scaffold creates routes like GET /api/Cards (line 405), POST /api/Cards (line 404), GET /api/Products/:id (line 436 implicit), PUT /api/Products/:id (line 437 via finale-rest default PUT handler). An authenticated user with access to card listing can read ALL users' card data via the auto-generated endpoint due to insufficient authorization on bulk read operations.
findings.recommendation: Replace finale-rest auto-scaffolding with explicit route definitions for each API endpoint. Define fine-grained authorization per resource (e.g., only allow Users to read their own Cards). Remove or restrict the Challenge model endpoint to prevent listing all challenges.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: server.ts:445-460 defines 14 auto-models with /api/${name}s endpoints. Lines 329-427 show the selective authorization overrides but the auto-scaffold still creates bulk read endpoints. This is a well-documented architectural weakness.

findings.title: 2FA TOTP secret returned in cleartext in API response during setup
findings.description: Route GET /rest/2fa/status at line 66 returns the TOTP secret in cleartext: res.json({ secret, ... }) where secret comes from otplib.authenticator.generateSecret() at line 64. An attacker who intercepts the 2FA setup response can capture the secret and bypass 2FA for the affected account.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Send GET /rest/2fa/status while logged in as an admin user who has 2FA configured. 2. The response returns the TOTP secret in the 'secret' field. 3. An attacker with network access or cookie access can use this secret to generate valid TOTP codes and bypass 2FA.
findings.recommendation: Do not return the TOTP secret in API responses. Instead, encode it in a QR code image or base32 string that the user must scan with an authenticator app. Store only a hash of the secret server-side. Return a QR code image URL instead of the raw secret.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: otplib.authenticator.generateSecret() at line 64 and direct return in res.json() at line 66. The secret is sent to the client and stored in the database (line 116). Network interception or token theft enables 2FA bypass.

findings.title: NoSQL injection in data export MongoDB query
findings.description: Route POST /rest/user/data-export at line 30 queries MongoDB with `db.ordersCollection.find({ email: updatedEmail })` where updatedEmail is derived from req.body.UserId (line 15-16). The email field is used directly in a MongoDB find query. While the email comes from the authenticated user's JWT claims (not directly from user input), the MongoDB find can still be manipulated if the token is forged (via F-004/F-005 vulnerabilities).
findings.exploit_scenario: If the attacker forges a JWT token (possible via F-004/F-005), they can set any email in the token claims. The data export then queries MongoDB for all orders matching that email. A noSQL $where or $regex injection could bypass authorization and access other users' orders.
findings.recommendation: Use strict type checking on MongoDB query operators. Filter the email field with a regex that only allows alphanumeric/valid email characters. Use parameterized query helpers or Sequelize ORM for MongoDB.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: MongoDB find() at line 30 with email derived from authenticated user claims. While the primary vector requires token forgery, the MongoDB query pattern is a known injection vector. Combined with JWT vulnerabilities, this is exploitable.

findings.title: Ethereum address comparison in checkKeys allows public key confusion
findings.description: Route POST /rest/web3/submitKey at line 18-27 compares req.body.privateKey against the derived private key and address. The error messages (lines 22-26) leak whether the attacker provided the address or public key, enabling step-by-step wallet enumeration. The mnemonic derivation at line 11 uses a hardcoded phrase.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Read the mnemonic from line 10. 2. Derive the private key using eth_utils. 3. Sweep all funds from the wallet. Alternatively, use the error messages to confirm whether a guessed address/public key is correct.
findings.recommendation: Remove the hardcoded mnemonic entirely. Do not expose different error messages for private key vs public key vs address mismatches — use a single error message for all cases.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: mnemonic at line 10, separate error messages at lines 22-26 leak which format the attacker entered. The hardcoded key makes this a trivial compromise.

findings.title: ZIP upload path traversal via unzipper entry validation
findings.description: Route POST /file-upload handles ZIP files at lines 38-45. The entry.path from unzipper is used to compute absolutePath at line 39 without validating individual path components. The guard at line 41 checks if absolutePath includes the resolve('.') base, but ZIP entry paths could still traverse via '..' components that resolve to directories outside uploads/complaints/. The check at line 41 is a substring check, not a path resolution comparison.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Create a ZIP file with entries containing '../..' in filenames (e.g., '../../ftp/legal.md'). 2. POST to /file-upload. 3. The unzipper.Parse() extracts the file at the computed path. While line 41 provides some protection, it checks for string inclusion of 'path.resolve('.')' which can be bypassed with crafted paths.
findings.recommendation: Validate each path component of entry.path before unwrapping. Replace '..' with '/' at every path level. Verify path.resolve('uploads/complaints/' + cleanedPath) === the expected base directory using path resolution comparison, not substring check.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: entry.path from unzipper used directly at line 39 with only a substring guard at line 41. ZIP entries can contain arbitrary paths. The path resolution comparison is insufficient against crafted traversal sequences.

findings.title: CSP bypass via user-controllable image URL in Content-Security-Policy header
findings.description: Route GET /profile at line 85 sets Content-Security-Policy header using user's profileImage URL: `script-src 'self' 'unsafe-eval' ${user?.profileImage}`. The profileImage URL (set via routes/updateUserProfile.ts or routes/profileImageUrlUpload.ts) becomes part of the CSP, allowing the user to inject script sources.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Set profileImage to '; script-src 'self' https://evil.com' via the profile image URL upload endpoint (routes/profileImageUrlUpload.ts:33). 2. When the profile page is rendered, the injected CSP allows scripts from evil.com. 3. The attacker serves a payload at evil.com that is now allowed by the permissive CSP.
findings.recommendation: Never include user input in CSP headers. Use hardcoded script-src directives or a CSP generator. Remove the user profileImage from the CSP construction entirely.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: CSP header construction at line 85 includes user?.profileImage which comes from user data. The fallback at routes/profileImageUrlUpload.ts:33 shows profileImage can be set to an arbitrary URL string via fetch() failure.

findings.title: Open redirect via String.includes() allowlist check
findings.description: Function isRedirectAllowed() at line 131 checks if the URL contains any allowlisted URL via String.includes(). The redirect endpoint at routes/redirect.ts:13 accepts a 'to' query parameter. An attacker can bypass the allowlist by embedding an allowed domain within a malicious URL, e.g., 'https://evil.com?redirect=https://allowed-domain.evil.com/phish'.
findings.exploit_scenario: Send GET /redirect?to=https://evil.com%3Fredirect%3Dhttps://explorer.dash.org/address/Xr556RzuwX6hg5EGpkybbv5RanJoZN17kW. The includes() check finds 'https://explorer.dash.org' within the string and allows the redirect to the evil.com domain, enabling phishing.
findings.recommendation: Parse the URL and check if the hostname exactly matches (or is a subdomain of) an allowlisted domain. Use a URL parsing library and validate the origin, not string containment.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: String.includes() at line 131 is used for URL allowlist checking. The allowlist entries are full URLs. Any URL containing an allowed URL as a substring passes. This is a well-known open redirect bypass pattern.

findings.title: Coupon system uses Z85 encoding (not encryption) — any discount coupon forgeable
findings.description: Function generateCoupon() at line 93-95 encodes a discount coupon using Z85 (base85 encoding, not encryption). The discountFromCoupon() at line 98-111 decodes it and validates the expiry format. Since Z85 is encoding not authentication, an attacker can generate arbitrary coupons: encode any desired discount and expiration month directly in Z85.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Pick a discount (e.g., 99) and a valid month (e.g., 'JAN26'). 2. Encode 'JAN26-99' in Z85 to get a valid coupon code. 3. POST to PUT /rest/basket/{id}/coupon/{encoded_coupon}. The system decodes and applies a 99% discount because it only checks the format and expiry, not the authenticity.
findings.recommendation: Sign the coupon using HMAC-SHA256 with a server-side secret key. The server should verify the HMAC before applying the discount. Store coupon IDs server-side for one-time use validation.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: z85.encode/decode at lines 93-115. Z85 is documented as 'encoding not encryption' in the code comments. The validation only checks expiry format (line 106), not a cryptographic signature. This is CWE-347 (unsafe cryptographic operation).

findings.title: Alchemy WebSocket API key hardcoded in source code
findings.description: Route POST /rest/web3/walletExploitAddress at line 18 connects to the Sepolia testnet using a hardcoded Alchemy WebSocket API key: 'wss://eth-sepolia.g.alchemy.com/v2/FZDapFZSs1l6yhHW4VnQqsi18qSd-3GJ'. This key is also present in routes/nftMint.ts:16. Anyone who reads the source can use this key for unauthorized blockchain RPC access.
findings.exploit_scenario: Extract the API key from web3Wallet.ts:18. Use it to make authenticated requests to the Alchemy JSON-RPC API, querying contract state, monitoring wallet balances, or performing other blockchain operations. Also extract the Alchemy key from nftMint.ts:16.
findings.recommendation: Store the API key in an environment variable (process.env.ALCHEMY_API_KEY) or a secrets manager. Never hardcode credentials in source code.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: Alchemy WebSocket URL with embedded API key at line 18 of routes/web3Wallet.ts and line 16 of routes/nftMint.ts. The key is used directly as a query parameter in the WebSocket URL.

findings.title: NoSQL injection in order history MongoDB query
findings.description: Route GET /rest/order-history at line 14 queries MongoDB with `ordersCollection.find({ email: updatedEmail })` where updatedEmail is derived from the authenticated user's email at line 13. While the email comes from JWT claims, the MongoDB find query could be manipulated with special characters if the email is improperly handled.
findings.exploit_scenario: If the JWT token is forged (via F-004/F-005), the attacker can inject MongoDB operators in the email field. Even without token forgery, the email is massaged via .replace(/[aeiou]/gi, '*') which does not sanitize against MongoDB operators.
findings.recommendation: Validate that the email field contains only valid email characters before using it in queries. Use strict equality or parameterized lookups for MongoDB queries.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: MongoDB find() at line 14 with email from JWT claims at line 13. The email transformation at line 13 only lowercases and star-vowels, which does not prevent NoSQL operators like $gt, $regex.

findings.title: Prometheus metrics endpoint exposes internal system metrics without authentication
findings.description: Route GET /metrics at line 63-72 serves Prometheus metrics via serveMetrics(). The middleware checks the User-Agent header (line 67-68) to determine if the user is monitored, but doesn't require authentication. The metrics include challenge progress, cheat scores, user counts, wallet balances, and order counts (lines 86-140).
findings.exploit_scenario: Send GET /metrics. The response includes metrics like juice_shop_wallet_balance_total (total wallet balances), juice_shop_users_registered (user counts by type), juice_shop_orders_placed_total (order counts), and challenge_solved metrics. An unauthenticated attacker can enumerate user population and financial data.
findings.recommendation: Add authentication middleware to the /metrics endpoint. Restrict access to admin/internal users only. Use IP-based filtering for internal monitoring.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: GET /metrics route at server.ts:662 with no authentication middleware. serveMetrics() at metrics.ts:63 serves register.metrics() without auth. The updateLoop at metrics.ts:142 computes sensitive financial/gamification metrics.

findings.title: Deluxe token generation uses HMAC with hardcoded private key
findings.description: Function deluxeToken() at lines 143-146 generates a deluxe token using HMAC-SHA256 with the RSA private key (which is itself hardcoded at line 17) as the HMAC key. The token is derived as HMAC(privateKey, email+deluxe). Since the key is known, any attacker can forge deluxe tokens for any email address.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Extract the RSA private key from line 17. 2. Compute HMAC-SHA256(privateKey, 'attacker@evil.comdeluxe') to generate a valid deluxe token. 3. Present the token to any /rest/deluxe-membership endpoint to get deluxe status.
findings.recommendation: Use a separate, randomly generated secret for deluxe token HMAC. Store it in an environment variable. Never use the RSA private key as an HMAC key.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: HMAC at line 144 uses the hardcoded privateKey (line 17) as the secret key. The input is predictable (email + 'deluxe'). Anyone with the source can compute valid deluxe tokens.

findings.title: Null byte bypass in file path allowlist enables access to restricted files
findings.description: Route GET /ftp/:file at line 25 applies cutOffPoisonNullByte() to strip '%00' from the filename before checking the allowlist. However, the null byte removal only happens after the allowlist check on line 24, and some paths that pass the allowlist check may still bypass with null bytes inserted at different positions in the filename.
findings.exploit_scenario: Send GET /ftp/incident-support.kdbx%00.pdf. The file is allowed because .pdf is in the allowlist (line 24). The null byte truncates the string before the extension reaches the server's file system, potentially serving unauthorized file types.
findings.recommendation: Apply null byte truncation first, then check the allowlist. Or better: use path.resolve() and verify the final path is within the intended directory. Validate the file extension of the actual resolved file, not the user-supplied input.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: cutOffPoisonNullByte at line 25 strips %00 but the allowlist check at line 24 happens before truncation. The serveIndex directory listing at server.ts:249 allows browsing all files in the /ftp directory.

findings.title: Stored XSS via bypassable sanitizeLegacy regex
findings.description:
Function sanitizeLegacy() at line 55 uses a weak regex /<(?:\w+)\W+?[\w]/gi to attempt HTML tag sanitization. The regex can be bypassed by using HTML tags without attributes (e.g., '
') or by using malformed tags that don't match the pattern. The sanitizeLegacy function is used in challenges mode (models/user.ts:46) to sanitize username and email fields.
findings.exploit_scenario:
In challenge mode (persistedXssUserChallenge), set username to '' or '
'. The legacy sanitization regex at line 55 allows through tags without attributes. These persist in the database and render as XSS when displayed.
findings.recommendation: Use the sanitizeSecure() function (line 57-64) in all code paths, which uses the sanitize-html library with proper allowlists. The sanitizeLegacy function should be removed entirely instead of being used in production.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: sanitizeLegacy regex at line 55 uses a permissive pattern that skips tags without attributes. Usage in models/user.ts:46 under challenge mode. The bypass of attributeless tag filtering is a known sanitization weakness.

findings.title: Service account default passwords hardcoded in login challenge validators
findings.description: Functions verifyPreLoginChallenges at lines 56-62 contain hardcoded password strings for various service accounts (admin@, support@, mc.safesearch@, amy@, etc.). While these are labeled as challenge validators, in a default configuration they represent weak/default passwords that could be used for account takeover.
findings.exploit_scenario: Send POST /rest/user/login with credentials: {email: 'admin@localhost', password: 'admin123'} (line 56). This authenticates as admin if the service accounts are used in the default deployment.
findings.recommendation: Do not embed default passwords in source code, even for challenges. Use unique, strong random passwords for all service accounts. Generate them at deployment time.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: lines 56-62 contain plaintext passwords for multiple service accounts. The regex patterns and expected values are literal strings in source code.

findings.title: Encryption key server lacks null byte processing enabling key file access
findings.description: Route GET /encryptionkeys/:file at line 11 serves files from the encryptionkeys directory without applying null byte truncation. The only protection is checking for '/' in the filename (line 10). An attacker can use null bytes to bypass extension checks on files that may have content type restrictions.
findings.exploit_scenario: Access GET /encryptionkeys/jwt.pub to retrieve the JWT public key (which is needed for the JWT algo confusion attack F-005). The serveIndex middleware at server.ts:256 also enables directory listing of the encryptionkeys folder.
findings.recommendation: Apply sanitization to the filename parameter. Use path.resolve() to verify the resolved path stays within the encryptionkeys directory. Restrict which files within the folder can be served.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: path.resolve('encryptionkeys/', file) at line 11 with no path normalization or null byte handling. serveIndex directory listing at server.ts:256 exposes all files. The file is used to serve the JWT public key which aids the JWT attack (F-005).

findings.title: Missing CSRF protection on state-changing endpoints
findings.description: Multiple POST/PUT/DELETE endpoints defined in server.ts at lines 549-618 (and earlier lines) do not include CSRF token verification. Routes include user login (line 549), reset-password (line 551), basket checkout (line 557), coupon application (line 558), 2FA operations (lines 422-439), and wallet operations (lines 579-580). The cookieParser middleware at line 266 provides session cookies but no anti-CSRF tokens.
findings.exploit_scenario: Craft a malicious HTML page that auto-submits a POST to /rest/basket/:id/checkout with an adversary-controlled basket ID. A logged-in victim visiting the page performs a checkout without their knowledge. Similarly, change payment methods or update profile settings via CSRF on unprotected endpoints.
findings.recommendation: Add CSRF tokens via the csurf middleware or SameSite cookie attributes on all state-changing endpoints. Alternatively, use custom header verification (check for a custom request header like X-CSRF-Token).
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: server.ts lines 549-618 show numerous POST/PUT routes without any CSRF middleware. express-ssrf is not imported, and the cookie at line 266 uses httponly but no SameSite is set by default. This matches CWE-352: Missing CSRF token.

findings.title: YAML Bomb via unbounded YAML deserialization in B2B upload
findings.description: Route POST /file-upload for YAML files at line 114 calls yaml.load(data) within a vm sandbox. While the sandbox has a 2-second timeout, an attacker can craft a YAML bomb (an extremely deep/broad YAML structure) that causes exponential memory growth (YamlBomb / billion laughs attack) before the timeout triggers.
findings.exploit_scenario: Send a YAML bomb via POST /file-upload with .yaml extension. The yaml.load() processes the document in memory, creating billions of references. The vm.runInContext timeout of 2s may not be enough to prevent the memory exhaustion from degrading server performance.
findings.recommendation: Use YAML.safeLoad() instead of yaml.load() to disable arbitrary object instantiation. Set a maximum document size limit before parsing. Use a YAML parser that limits nested object depth by default.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: yaml.load() at line 114 with untrusted data loaded into the JS-YAML parser. The 2s timeout at line 114 is the only mitigation. YAML.load() (vs safeLoad) is known to enable arbitrary object creation and memory exhaustion.

findings.title: Server-Side Request Forgery via profile image URL upload
findings.description: Route POST /profile/image/url at line 21 accepts a user-sup imageUrl from req.body and uses fetch(url) to retrieve it, without validating the URL protocol, host, or internal network addresses. If fetch fails (line 31-38), the raw URL is stored as profileImage, creating a persistent SSRF indicator. The SSRF check at line 17 only looks for specific challenge patterns, not preventing SSRF broadly.
findings.exploit_scenario: Send POST /profile/image/url with body: {"imageUrl": "http://localhost:3000/.well-known/security.txt"}. The fetch retrieves internal files. If fetch is made to an internal-only service, the server acts as a proxy, enabling internal network enumeration, cloud metadata access, or port scanning.
findings.recommendation: Validate the URL scheme (only https/http), reject private IP ranges (10.x, 172.16-31.x, 192.168.x, 127.x, 169.254.x, ::1), and use DNS resolution to verify the hostname resolves to a non-private IP before fetching.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: fetch(url) at line 21 with no URL validation on the protocol or host. The URL comes from req.body.imageUrl (line 16). The catch block at line 31 directly stores the user-supplied URL without any sanitization.

findings.title: Potential code execution in B2B order via noEval sandbox
findings.description: Route POST /b2b/v2/orders at line 20 uses vm.runInContext('safeEval(orderLinesData)', sandbox) where orderLinesData is user input. The safeEval (notevil) is meant to prevent code execution by stripping dangerous globals (process, require, etc.), but notevil may be bypassed depending on the specific version. The 2-second timeout at line 20 is the only hard mitigation.
findings.exploit_scenario: If the noEval sandbox is bypassed (e.g., via prototype pollution or environment variable access), an attacker can execute arbitrary Node.js code: {orderLinesData: 'process.mainModule.require("child_process").execSync("id").toString()'}. The timeout prevents infinite loops but not fast code execution.
findings.recommendation: Validate orderLinesData as structured JSON only, not arbitrary JavaScript. Parse it with JSON.parse() and validate each field. Remove the VM sandbox if it's not needed — if parsing is required, use a safe deserializer.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: vm.runInContext with user-controlled orderLinesData at line 20. The notevil safeEval is a sandbox mechanism that may have bypass vectors depending on the Node.js version and library characteristics.

findings.title: CTF key loaded from plaintext file on disk
findings.description: The ctfFlag() function at lines 85-90 loads the CTF HMAC key from ctf.key file via fs.readFileSync() at line 79. If process.env.CTF_KEY is not set, the file contents are used. The file is included in the repository and may be world-readable.
findings.exploit_scenario: Read the ctf.key file from the project root. Use the key with fs.readFileSync at line 79 (or process.env.CTF_KEY if set). The key is used to compute HMAC-SHA256 for CTF challenge flags, allowing an attacker to generate valid flags for any challenge.
findings.recommendation: Always use an environment variable for the CTF key. Never include the key file in the repository. Use different keys per deployment.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: fs.readFileSync('ctf.key', 'utf8') at line 79. The key is literally in the source directory. Used for HMAC signing of CTF flags.

findings.title: Directory listing exposes sensitive files in FTP, encryptionkeys, and logs directories
findings.description: Routes at lines 249-261 of server.ts configure serveIndex directory listing for /ftp, /encryptionkeys, and /support/logs without authentication. serveIndex renders all files in each directory, allowing enumeration of backup files, encryption keys, and log files.
findings.exploit_scenario: Send GET /encryptionkeys/ to enumerate all encryption key files. Send GET /ftp/ to list FTP files including backup files .bak, .pyc, .md. Send GET /support/logs/ to list server log files. Accessing any listed file directly (e.g., GET /encryptionkeys/jwt.pub) serves its contents.
findings.recommendation: Remove serveIndex directory listing middleware from sensitive directories. Implement proper file listing with server-side filtering, or require authentication for any directory browse access.
findings.confidence_reason: Direct code evidence: serveIndex('ftp' at server.ts:249, serveIndex('encryptionkeys' at server.ts:256, serveIndex('logs' at server.ts:259. All have icons: true enabling full file listing. The robots.txt at line 211 disallows /ftp but that directive is advisory only.
